title: Pyrography lettering
subtitle: Writing text onto wood with a writing tip or flat skew tip
excerpt: Lettering is the most useful entry point in pyrography: a name, a date, or a short inscription burned into a wooden piece is useful and readable, and the writing tip gives enough control for a confident beginner. The key is consistent pressure and consistent tip angle; the result then depends on timing and practice rather than artistic skill. This beginner technique covers the heat setting, the two-stroke letter construction, and the layout method for centred text.
sourceNotes: Handicraft for Girls (1914) by Idabelle McGlauflin for pyrography lettering exercises. The Art Amateur vol. 47 (1902) for letter forms and inscription technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pyrography nib with a fine point used for line work and lettering. Produces a consistent thin line when moved at a consistent speed.
The heated tool used in pyrography. A solid-tip burner is simpler to use for lettering; a wire-nib burner gives finer temperature control for detailed work.

content.text: Pyrography lettering produces a permanent, legible inscription with no paint, no ink, and no secondary process. A solid-tip
burner
at a medium heat with a
writing tip

is the simplest setup and produces clear results on sycamore, lime, and birch. The letters are drawn onto the surface lightly in pencil first, then burned over; the pencil lines burn away with the first pass, leaving only the burned line.
What it does

A writing tip moved at a consistent speed across a pencil letter outline burns a fine dark line into the wood surface. Multiple passes make the line darker and wider; a single pass at medium heat on light wood produces a warm mid-brown line.
When to use it
For any inscription, name, date, or short text on a wooden piece. The writing tip is also used for outlines and fine detail work in pictorial pyrography.
What you need

A solid-tip pyrography burner with a writing tip. A cooling stand. A pencil and ruler for layout. A wood surface sanded to 320 grit before burning. The pencil lines must be light; heavy pencil lines burn into the wood and can leave a mark alongside the intended burned line.
Safety
Work with ventilation; burning wood produces smoke. Rest the tip on the cooling stand between passes.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Layout

Draw two parallel pencil guide lines the height of the intended lettering. A capital letter height of 10 to 12 mm is comfortable for a first attempt and readable at arm's length. Write the text lightly in pencil between the lines, using a simple printed hand rather than cursive; each letter is clearest when its strokes do not connect to the adjacent letters.
Step 2: Set the heat

Heat the solid-tip burner and test on scrap. At the correct heat for sycamore or lime, a medium-speed stroke produces a warm mid-brown line. If the test line is too pale at medium speed, add a fraction more heat. Too much heat produces a black line on the first pass; useful for emphasising a specific letter but distracting when uniform.
Step 3: Burn the strokes

Burn each letter in separate strokes rather than in a continuous movement. A printed letter A has three strokes (left diagonal, right diagonal, crossbar); burn each stroke as a separate movement from start to finish. Move the tip at a steady pace, keeping the same contact pressure throughout. The pencil guide lines burn away during this pass; the burned lines replace them.
Step 4: Erase pencil and finish

Once cooled, any remaining pencil marks can be erased gently with a soft eraser; use light pressure only to avoid abrading the burned surface. Brush off all dust before applying any finish. A light coat of Danish oil deepens the wood colour and makes the burned lettering stand out more strongly against a darker background.
What it looks like when it is right

Clean, consistent-width strokes with no thick-and-thin variation (unless calligraphy variation is intended). Even mid-brown tone throughout the text. Guide lines are gone; the burned letters stand cleanly against the pale wood background.
Common mistakes
Dark blobs where strokes start or end: the tip paused before moving. Start the movement before the tip touches the wood and lift off before stopping.
Letters are too faint: the heat was too low or the speed too fast. Increase the heat slightly and move more slowly; on a second pass, a slow stroke over a faint line will darken it.
